title: Psalms 125
subtitle: Those Who Trust in the LORD Are Like Mount Zion

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Song of Ascent

heb.paragraph: "Those who trust in the LORD are like Mount Zion (har), which cannot be shaken but endures forever. As the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds his people both now and forevermore" (vv.1–2). The geography of trust: the pilgrim arriving in Jerusalem sees the surrounding mountains and draws the analogy: as these hills surround the city, God surrounds his people. The sceptre of the wicked will not remain over the righteous (v.3).

notes.note: "Like Mount Zion, which cannot be shaken. As the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds his people." The unshakable mountain and the surrounding mountains: two geographic truths as theology.
"The sceptre of the wicked will not remain over the land allotted to the righteous." Wicked rule is temporary. The land belongs to the righteous; oppression will not last.

refs.note: The wise builder's house on rock stands firm against storm—immovable like Mount Zion.
God will shake heaven and earth, but we receive an unshakeable kingdom.
Those God foreknew he predestined, called, justified, glorified—secure in his purpose.

hist.context: Psalm 125 uses Zion's geography as spiritual metaphor. Just as mountains surround Jerusalem (to the east, north, and south), so the LORD surrounds his people. Those who trust in YHWH are like Mount Zion itself—immovable, enduring forever. This promise addressed exilic and post-exilic anxiety: would Israel survive among the nations? Yes, as surely as Zion stands.

heb.paragraph: "Peace (shālōm) be on Israel" (v.5). God does good to the good and upright (v.4). Those who turn to crooked ways will be banished with the evildoers (v.5). The psalm ends with the shalom blessing — the third time shalom closes a Song of Ascent (120:7; 122:8; 125:5). The pilgrimage moves from war (120) through prayer-for-peace (122) to bestowed peace (125).

notes.note: "LORD, do good to those who are good. But those who turn to crooked ways the LORD will banish. Peace be on Israel." The binary: good → good; crooked → banished. Then: peace on the whole. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: Peace and mercy on the Israel of God—Paul's blessing echoes the psalm's closing.
Whoever sows injustice reaps calamity—the scepter of wickedness will not remain.
God will pay back trouble to troublers and give relief to the troubled.

hist.context: The psalm distinguishes between the righteous and those who 'turn to crooked ways.' The 'scepter of the wicked' will not remain over the land allotted to the righteous—foreign or corrupt rule is temporary. The closing prayer for peace upon Israel anticipates the climactic Psalm 128's blessing. The Ascent collection repeatedly assures pilgrims: despite present troubles, Zion's God secures his people's future.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is not mentioned in Psalm 125 and is not a key figure in this particular psalm.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in Those Who Trust in the LORD Are Like Mount Zion.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.text: Solomon is not mentioned in Psalm 125 and is not a key figure in this psalm.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVLord, do good to those who are good, to those who are upright in heart.ESVDo good, O LORD, to those who are good, and to those who are upright in their hearts!NIVBut those who turn to crooked ways the Lord will banish with the evildoers. Peace be on Israel.ESVBut those who turn aside to their crooked ways the LORD will lead away with evildoers! Peace be upon Israel!

rows.label: Peace on Israel — 125:4–5
rows.text: Good to the good. Crooked banished. "PEACE be on Israel." Third shalom in the Ascents.

hebtext.note: "Those who trust in the LORD are like Mount Zion (har), which cannot be shaken but endures forever. As the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds his people both now and forevermore" (vv.1–2). The geography of trust: the pilgrim arriving in Jerusalem sees the surrounding mountains and draws the analogy: as these hills surround the city, God surrounds his people. The sceptre of the wicked will not remain over the righteous (v.3).

"Peace (shālōm) be on Israel" (v.5). God does good to the good and upright (v.4). Those who turn to crooked ways will be banished with the evildoers (v.5). The psalm ends with the shalom blessing — the third time shalom closes a Song of Ascent (120:7; 122:8; 125:5). The pilgrimage moves from war (120) through prayer-for-peace (122) to bestowed peace (125).

themes.note: Faith peaks: trust as unshakable as Zion. Sovereignty: the LORD surrounds like mountains. Geography as theology.

prayer_prompt: Those who trust in the LORD are like Mount Zion — unshaken forever. Consider asking God about your stability — is it founded on trusting Him or on managing circumstances?
